words.simpleDefinition: To explain something so clearly that anyone can follow what you mean.
words.longDefinition: To articulate is to put thoughts into precise, well-formed words. It implies not just speaking, but speaking with structure — connecting ideas so the listener can build a complete picture.
words.professionalContext: Senior engineers articulate trade-offs in design docs so non-technical stakeholders can weigh in confidently.
words.exampleSentence: She articulated the migration risks in three bullet points the executive team could act on.
words.etymology: From Latin articulatus, 'jointed, distinct' — like joints linking bones, articulate speech links ideas distinctly.
words.connector: Like a clear radio signal — every syllable lands cleanly with no static.

words.simpleDefinition: Saying exactly what's needed using as few words as possible.
words.longDefinition: Concise communication strips away filler, redundancy, and hedge words while preserving the core meaning. It respects the reader's time by saying only what must be said.
words.professionalContext: Pull request descriptions should be concise — one paragraph explaining the change, not three pages of meandering context.
words.exampleSentence: His concise summary of the outage saved the on-call team an hour of context-gathering.
words.etymology: From Latin concisus, 'cut short' — the same root as scissors. Concise prose is cut down to its essence.
words.connector: Like a haiku — every word earns its place.

words.simpleDefinition: Saying what you really think, even if it's uncomfortable.
words.longDefinition: Candor means truthful expression without softening or evasion. In healthy teams, candid feedback is given with care for the person but no compromise on the message.
words.professionalContext: A candid retrospective surfaces what actually went wrong, not just what's safe to discuss.
words.exampleSentence: Her candid assessment of the architecture's flaws led to a much-needed rewrite.
words.etymology: From Latin candidus, 'white, pure' — candid means unclouded by spin or politics.
words.connector: Like a window with no curtains — what you see is what's actually there.

words.simpleDefinition: Talking or writing way more than necessary to get the point across.
words.longDefinition: Verbose communication buries the message under unnecessary detail, qualifications, or repetition. Often a sign of unclear thinking — the writer hasn't yet found the one true sentence.
words.professionalContext: Verbose error messages help debugging, but verbose code reviews exhaust the author and dilute the actionable feedback.
words.exampleSentence: The spec was so verbose that no one read past page two.
words.etymology: From Latin verbosus, 'wordy' — literally 'full of words'.
words.connector: Like a five-paragraph email when one sentence would do.

words.shortDefinition: open to more than one interpretation
words.simpleDefinition: Could mean two different things — and you can't tell which.
words.longDefinition: Ambiguous statements leave room for multiple valid readings. In specs and APIs, ambiguity is a defect — it forces readers to guess intent, leading to inconsistent implementations.
words.professionalContext: Ambiguous requirements are how three engineers build three different things from the same ticket.
words.exampleSentence: 'Make it faster' is too ambiguous — faster how, and by how much?
words.etymology: From Latin ambiguus, 'going both ways' — ambi (both) + agere (to drive).
words.connector: Like a road sign pointing in two directions at once.

words.simpleDefinition: Short, clear, and to the point — nothing wasted.
words.longDefinition: Succinct combines brevity with completeness. Unlike just 'short', succinct implies the message is also clear and self-contained — no follow-up questions needed.
words.professionalContext: A succinct status update tells the team where things stand without requiring a meeting.
words.exampleSentence: His succinct summary of the proposal fit on a sticky note.
words.etymology: From Latin succinctus, 'girded up' — like tucking up loose clothing for action.
words.connector: Like an executive summary that actually summarizes.

words.shortDefinition: the quality of being clear and easy to understand
words.simpleDefinition: How easy something is to understand on the first read.
words.longDefinition: Clarity is the absence of friction between intent and comprehension. In writing, code, and design, it's the highest form of craft — making complex things feel simple.
words.professionalContext: Clarity in design docs prevents weeks of rework — readers either understand the proposal or they don't.
words.exampleSentence: The diagram brought instant clarity to a system she'd been confused about for months.
words.etymology: From Latin clarus, 'clear, bright' — same root as 'clear' itself.
words.connector: Like looking through clean glass instead of a foggy windshield.

words.simpleDefinition: Keeping things short on purpose.
words.longDefinition: Brevity is the discipline of saying only what matters. As Shakespeare put it: 'brevity is the soul of wit' — meaning clever expression depends on tight, uncluttered language.
words.professionalContext: Brevity in commit messages helps future engineers scan history without drowning in prose.
words.exampleSentence: She prized brevity over completeness in her one-on-one notes.
words.etymology: From Latin brevitas, 'shortness' — same root as 'brief'.
words.connector: Like a tweet — the constraint forces the point.

words.simpleDefinition: Insider words that only people in your field understand.
words.longDefinition: Jargon serves as efficient shorthand inside a community but becomes a barrier when used with outsiders. Mature communicators code-switch — using jargon with peers, plain language with everyone else.
words.professionalContext: Cutting jargon from product specs helps PMs, designers, and engineers stay aligned on the same terms.
words.exampleSentence: He drowned the customer in jargon — words like 'idempotent' and 'eventual consistency' that meant nothing to them.
words.etymology: From Old French jargoun, 'chatter of birds' — meaningless to outsiders.
words.connector: Like a secret handshake — efficient inside the group, alienating outside it.

words.simpleDefinition: When two people just 'click' and conversation flows naturally.
words.longDefinition: Rapport is the unspoken trust and ease that develops between people who understand each other's communication style. It makes hard conversations possible and easy conversations effortless.
words.professionalContext: Building rapport with your tech lead in your first weeks pays dividends when you later need to push back on a design.
words.exampleSentence: Their easy rapport made the architecture review feel like a brainstorm rather than a debate.
words.etymology: From French rapporter, 'to bring back' — implying mutual exchange.
words.connector: Like the handshake protocol two systems use to establish a connection.

words.shortDefinition: give a brief statement of the main points
words.simpleDefinition: Distill something long into the key points anyone could repeat.
words.longDefinition: To summarize is to compress without losing essence — keeping the load-bearing claims and discarding examples, asides, and detail. A good summary lets someone act on the content without reading the original.
words.professionalContext: Engineers who can summarize a 30-minute design discussion in three bullet points become trusted operators.
words.exampleSentence: Can you summarize the postmortem in two paragraphs for the leadership digest?
words.etymology: From Latin summa, 'sum, top, total' — to summarize is to give the totals.
words.connector: Like a chapter title — captures the chapter without being it.

words.shortDefinition: express the same idea in different words
words.simpleDefinition: Saying what someone else said, but in your own words.
words.longDefinition: Paraphrasing reformulates an idea using different language while preserving the original meaning. It's a key check for understanding — if you can't paraphrase someone, you didn't really hear them.
words.professionalContext: In a 1:1, paraphrasing what your manager said back to them confirms alignment before you commit to action.
words.exampleSentence: Let me paraphrase to make sure I follow — you want us to ship the feature behind a flag, then enable it for 1% of users?
words.etymology: From Greek paraphrasis, 'beside the phrase' — restating alongside the original.
words.connector: Like translating between two dialects of the same language.

words.simpleDefinition: Add more detail to something you've already mentioned.
words.longDefinition: To elaborate is to expand a point with detail, examples, or implications. It's the inverse of 'summarize' — moving from headline to full picture.
words.professionalContext: When a stakeholder says 'we need this to be more secure', the right move is to ask them to elaborate before designing.
words.exampleSentence: She asked him to elaborate on what 'real-time' meant — milliseconds, seconds, or minutes?
words.etymology: From Latin elaboratus, 'worked out' — labor (work) extended into careful detail.
words.connector: Like zooming in on a map — same territory, more detail.

words.shortDefinition: fluent and persuasive in speaking or writing
words.simpleDefinition: Speaking so beautifully and clearly that it moves people.
words.longDefinition: Eloquence combines clarity, rhythm, and emotional resonance. An eloquent speaker doesn't just convey information — they make the listener care about it.
words.professionalContext: An eloquent engineering all-hands talk turns a dry quarterly update into something the whole team remembers.
words.exampleSentence: Her eloquent defense of the rewrite shifted skeptical exec opinion overnight.
words.etymology: From Latin eloqui, 'to speak out' — 'e-' (out) + 'loqui' (to speak).
words.connector: Like music that happens to be made of words.

words.shortDefinition: showing subtle distinctions or shades of meaning
words.simpleDefinition: Showing the small but important differences between things.
words.longDefinition: A nuanced view rejects black-and-white framings and acknowledges the gradients between extremes. Nuanced thinking is a hallmark of senior engineers — recognizing that 'it depends' is often the most accurate answer.
words.professionalContext: A nuanced architecture review weighs trade-offs explicitly rather than declaring one approach 'right'.
words.exampleSentence: Her nuanced take on monoliths vs microservices won over both camps.
words.etymology: From French nuance, 'shade of color' — itself from Latin nubes, 'cloud'. Nuance shades the picture.
words.connector: Like the gradient between two colors — not just one or the other.

words.simpleDefinition: The thing that stands out as most important.
words.longDefinition: Salient details are the ones that demand attention — the load-bearing facts in an argument, the key signals in noisy data. Naming what's salient is a form of leadership.
words.professionalContext: The salient point of the postmortem wasn't the bug itself — it was the missing alert that let it run for hours.
words.exampleSentence: She cut twelve minor concerns and led with the one salient risk: data loss.
words.etymology: From Latin saliens, 'leaping' — what leaps out at you.
words.connector: Like the bright dot on a radar screen.

words.simpleDefinition: An argument so well-reasoned it's hard to disagree with.
words.longDefinition: Cogent reasoning chains evidence and logic so tightly that the conclusion feels inevitable. Different from 'eloquent' (beautiful) — cogent is structurally airtight.
words.professionalContext: His cogent case for the migration was three slides: cost today, cost in 18 months, and the inflection point.
words.exampleSentence: Her cogent rebuttal closed the debate in under five minutes.
words.etymology: From Latin cogens, 'compelling, forcing together'. Cogent reasoning compels assent.
words.connector: Like a mathematical proof — each step follows from the last.

words.simpleDefinition: Very short — sometimes so short it feels cold.
words.longDefinition: Terse goes beyond concise — it's brevity with an edge. Used in code reviews ('LGTM') it's efficient; used in interpersonal moments it can feel curt.
words.professionalContext: His terse one-line PR comments saved time but built a reputation for being hard to read in tone.
words.exampleSentence: Her terse 'we'll discuss' email made everyone nervous about the meeting.
words.etymology: From Latin tersus, 'wiped clean, polished' — terse prose has been wiped of all extras.
words.connector: Like a Unix command — does the job, no pleasantries.

words.simpleDefinition: So clearly explained you can almost see it.
words.longDefinition: Lucid writing makes the reader feel smarter than they are — every paragraph leaves them with new clarity. Often used to describe technical writing that demystifies complex topics.
words.professionalContext: A lucid architecture diagram replaces hours of explanation.
words.exampleSentence: His lucid explanation of OAuth2 became the team's onboarding doc.
words.etymology: From Latin lucidus, 'shining, clear' — same root as 'illuminate'.
words.connector: Like sunlight in a clean room — nothing hidden.

words.shortDefinition: logical and consistent; forming a unified whole
words.simpleDefinition: All the parts hang together and tell one consistent story.
words.longDefinition: Coherent communication has internal consistency — the parts support each other and don't contradict. A coherent strategy means today's decisions don't undo tomorrow's.
words.professionalContext: A coherent technical roadmap shows how each quarter's work builds toward a clear destination.
words.exampleSentence: The deck was visually striking but not coherent — slide three undermined slide one.
words.etymology: From Latin cohaerere, 'to stick together' — same root as 'cohesion'.
words.connector: Like a well-structured codebase where every module fits the architecture.

words.shortDefinition: able to convince others to take action
words.simpleDefinition: So convincing it changes how someone thinks or acts.
words.longDefinition: Persuasion combines logic, emotion, and credibility. In engineering, persuasion is how technical correctness becomes organizational change — being right isn't enough.
words.professionalContext: A persuasive RFC includes the cost of inaction, not just the benefits of the proposal.
words.exampleSentence: Her persuasive case for the rewrite secured the budget on the first ask.
words.etymology: From Latin persuadere, 'to advise thoroughly' — per- (thoroughly) + suadere (to urge).
words.connector: Like a magnet that draws agreement.

words.shortDefinition: relating to the art of effective speaking
words.simpleDefinition: Stylistic — designed for effect rather than literal answer.
words.longDefinition: Rhetorical can mean persuasive in style, or it can mean a question asked for effect rather than answer ('isn't that obvious?'). Knowing both meanings keeps you from misreading the room.
words.professionalContext: A rhetorical 'do we really need another microservice?' from a principal engineer is feedback, not a question.
words.exampleSentence: His rhetorical flourishes made the all-hands speech memorable.
words.etymology: From Greek rhetorikos, 'pertaining to public speaking' — the art of the orator.
words.connector: Like a spotlight — calls attention without demanding response.

words.shortDefinition: a memory aid, often a phrase or pattern
words.simpleDefinition: A trick that helps you remember something hard.
words.longDefinition: Mnemonics encode hard-to-remember information into easier patterns — acronyms, rhymes, vivid images. SOLID, CAP, ACID are all mnemonic acronyms that compress complex ideas into recall-friendly form.
words.professionalContext: Engineering loves mnemonics: SOLID (design principles), DRY (don't repeat yourself), KISS (keep it simple).
words.exampleSentence: The CAP theorem mnemonic — Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance — is how most engineers first internalize it.
words.etymology: From Greek mnemonikos, 'pertaining to memory' — same root as Mnemosyne, Greek goddess of memory.
words.connector: Like a shortcut key — small effort, big recall.

words.simpleDefinition: Related, but not really to the point at hand.
words.longDefinition: Tangential remarks touch the topic at one point and then veer off. In meetings, tangents can spark good ideas — but unchecked, they devour time and obscure decisions.
words.professionalContext: Calling out 'that's tangential, let's park it' is a small skill that keeps meetings on track.
words.exampleSentence: His comment about Rust was tangential to the deploy discussion but worth a follow-up.
words.etymology: From Latin tangens, 'touching' — a tangent line touches a curve at one point only.
words.connector: Like a side road off the highway — connected, but not the route.

words.simpleDefinition: So obviously true it doesn't need explaining.
words.longDefinition: Axiomatic statements are the unquestioned starting points of an argument. Recognizing what your team treats as axiomatic — and questioning it occasionally — is how you discover stale assumptions.
words.professionalContext: It was treated as axiomatic that the database must be relational — until someone asked why, and the answer turned out to be 'because we always have'.
words.exampleSentence: She challenged the axiomatic belief that microservices were always better than monoliths.
words.etymology: From Greek axiōma, 'that which is thought worthy' — an axiom is what's worthy of being assumed.
words.connector: Like the rules of chess — you don't argue them, you play them.
